title: Introduction to aRtpalette package
text: person loves interested art, often find struggling bring colors artwork paintings data visualizations R. process frustrating, requiring manual look-hex codes, copying one one, figuring apply ggplot2 chart. clean way store palette table, visualize using , expand needed colors original artwork provided. frustration motivated build aRtpalette. goal package bring user small toolkit makes working art-inspired Color palettes R feel natural streamlined. wanted able store colors structured object, plot swatches preview working , expand palette creatively, chart needed colors original painting contained. vignette walks three core functions package shows work together realistic workflow using built-artwork_palettes dataset.

title: Introduction to aRtpalette package
text: built-dataset table 20 palettes extracted iconic artworks across art history, sourced Color Lisa. row one artwork, palette contains five hex color codes alongside metadata like artist name art movement. kind structured color table always wanted available R. can filter style, browse artist, pull row immediately start working colors.
code: data(artwork_palettes) head(artwork_palettes) #>        palette_name                        artwork   artist    hex1    hex2 #> 1      starry_night               The Starry Night Van Gogh #1a3431 #2b41a7 #> 2     bedroom_arles               Bedroom in Arles Van Gogh #374D8D #93A0CB #> 3 self_portrait_hat Self-Portrait with a Straw Hat Van Gogh #FBDC30 #A7A651 #> 4        great_wave    The Great Wave off Kanagawa  Hokusai #1F284C #2D4472 #> 5      water_lilies            Water Lilies (1906)    Monet #9F4640 #4885A4 #> 6     woman_parasol           Woman with a Parasol    Monet #82A4BC #4C7899 #>      hex3    hex4    hex5              style #> 1 #6283c8 #ccc776 #c7ad24 Post-Impressionism #> 2 #82A866 #C4B743 #A35029 Post-Impressionism #> 3 #E0BA7A #9BA7B0 #5A5F80 Post-Impressionism #> 4 #6E6352 #D9CCAC #ECE2C6            Ukiyo-e #> 5 #395A92 #7EA860 #B985BA      Impressionism #> 6 #2F5136 #B1B94C #E5DCBE      Impressionism # How many art styles are represented? table(artwork_palettes$style) #>  #>        Art Nouveau            Baroque             Cubism           De Stijl  #>                  1                  2                  1                  1  #>   Dutch Golden Age      Expressionism      Impressionism        Pointillism  #>                  1                  1                  2                  1  #>            Pop Art Post-Impressionism        Regionalism        Renaissance  #>                  1                  3                  1                  2  #>         Surrealism            Ukiyo-e  #>                  2                  1

what: Step 1: Creating a palette object with palette_from_hex()
title: Introduction to aRtpalette package
text: first function, palette_from_hex(), takes vector hex color codes name, returns validated palette object class “artpalette”. function checks every hex code properly formatted proceeding, get clear error message instead silent failure later workflow. Let’s use Van Gogh’s Starry Night dataset create palette object . result list class “artpalette” stores name color codes. object two functions expect input. output central unit package. function catch bad input immediately:
code: row <- artwork_palettes[artwork_palettes$palette_name == "starry_night", ]  starry_night <- palette_from_hex(   hex_codes = c(row$hex1, row$hex2, row$hex3, row$hex4, row$hex5),   name      = "starry_night" )  starry_night #> $name #> [1] "starry_night" #>  #> $colors #> [1] "#1a3431" "#2b41a7" "#6283c8" "#ccc776" "#c7ad24" #>  #> attr(,"class") #> [1] "artpalette" # This fails gracefully with a clear message palette_from_hex(c("not_a_hex", "#FF5733"), name = "bad_palette") #> Error in `palette_from_hex()`: #> ! Invalid hex codes:not_a_hex

what: Step 2: Visualizing a palette with plot_palette()
title: Introduction to aRtpalette package
text: using palette chart, helps see . second function, plot_palette(), takes "artpalette" object renders clean color swatch grid showing color block hex code. store preview step always wished existed.  can immediately see deep navy blues warm gold tones define painting. visual check applying palette real data one useful parts workflow. function removed process go back forth, applying colors, liking , start . Let’s also preview palettes dataset compare styles:   Side side, three palettes already tell story different art movements use color. Impressionism leans toward softness naturalness, Pop Art bold high-contrast.
code: plot_palette(starry_night) # Monet's Water Lilies row2 <- artwork_palettes[artwork_palettes$palette_name == "water_lilies", ] water_lilies <- palette_from_hex(   c(row2$hex1, row2$hex2, row2$hex3, row2$hex4, row2$hex5),   name = "water_lilies" ) plot_palette(water_lilies) # Warhol's Marilyn Monroe row3 <- artwork_palettes[artwork_palettes$palette_name == "marilyn_warhol", ] marilyn <- palette_from_hex(   c(row3$hex1, row3$hex2, row3$hex3, row3$hex4, row3$hex5),   name = "marilyn_warhol" ) plot_palette(marilyn)

what: Step 3: Expanding a palette with apply_palette()
title: Introduction to aRtpalette package
text: third function,apply_palette(), takes "artpalette" object number n, returns exactly n hex color codes ready use ggplot2 chart. ask colors palette contains, function interpolates existing colors using R’s built-color ramp. user can take 5-color painting palette expand 10, 15, 20 colors large categorical chart. mean “expand original palette creativity.” source painting gives anchor colors, function handles rest.
code: # Get exactly 3 colors from the Starry Night palette three_colors <- apply_palette(starry_night, n = 3) three_colors #> [1] "#1A3431" "#6283C8" "#C7AD24" # Expand to 10 colors — interpolating between the original 5 ten_colors <- apply_palette(starry_night, n = 10) ten_colors #>  [1] "#1A3431" "#213965" "#293F99" "#3D56B2" "#5574C0" "#7992B5" "#A8B091" #>  [8] "#CBC46C" "#C9B848" "#C7AD24"

title: Introduction to aRtpalette package
text: full example using artwork_palettes three functions working together. count many artworks belong art style dataset visualize using Starry Night palette.   chart built entirely artwork_palettes. style groups came style column, bar heights came counting rows, colors came expanding Starry Night palette using apply_palette(). three functions worked together one clean pipeline.
code: # Step 1: Pull the Starry Night palette from our dataset row_sn <- artwork_palettes[artwork_palettes$palette_name == "starry_night", ]  pal_sn <- palette_from_hex( hex_codes = c(row_sn$hex1, row_sn$hex2, row_sn$hex3, row_sn$hex4, row_sn$hex5),   name = "starry_night" ) # Step 2: Preview it before using it plot_palette(pal_sn) # Step 3: Count artworks per art style in artwork_palettes style_counts <- as.data.frame(table(artwork_palettes$style)) colnames(style_counts) <- c("style", "count")  # Step 4: Expand the palette to match the number of style groups chart_colors <- apply_palette(pal_sn, n = nrow(style_counts))  # Step 5: Build the chart using our own data ggplot(style_counts, aes(x = reorder(style, count),                           y = count,                           fill = style)) +   geom_bar(stat = "identity", width = 0.7) +   scale_fill_manual(values = chart_colors) +   coord_flip() +   labs(     title    = "Number of Artworks by Art Style",     subtitle = "Colors from Van Gogh's The Starry Night",     x        = "Art Style",     y        = "Number of Artworks"   ) +   theme_classic() +   theme(legend.position = "none")

what: Creative expansion: going beyond the original palette
title: Introduction to aRtpalette package
text: apply_palette() function becomes useful need colors original painting provides. take Klimt’s Kiss, 5-color palette, use function expand 15 colors, showing function interpolates original colors staying true painting’s spirit.   “expanding original palette creativity” looks like practice: source painting gives anchor colors, apply_palette() handles rest.
code: # Start with Klimt's The Kiss (5 colors) row_k <- artwork_palettes[artwork_palettes$palette_name == "kiss_klimt", ] pal_k <- palette_from_hex(   c(row_k$hex1, row_k$hex2, row_k$hex3, row_k$hex4, row_k$hex5),   name = "kiss_klimt" ) plot_palette(pal_k) # Expand to 15 interpolated colors expanded <- apply_palette(pal_k, n = 15)  expanded_df <- data.frame(   x     = seq_len(15),   color = expanded )  ggplot(expanded_df, aes(x = x, y = 1, fill = color)) +   geom_tile(width = 0.95, height = 0.8) +   scale_fill_identity() +   labs(     title    = "Klimt's The Kiss — expanded from 5 to 15 colors",     subtitle = "apply_palette() interpolates between the original palette colors"   ) +   theme_void() +   theme(plot.title    = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 12),         plot.subtitle = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 10))

title: Introduction to aRtpalette package
text: three functions aRtpalette designed work pipeline: palette_from_hex() — store hex codes artwork validated, named palette object plot_palette() — preview palette color swatches applying chart apply_palette() — extract expand palette exactly number colors chart needs.

title: Art-Inspired Color Palette Tools
text: can install development version artpalette GitHub :
code: # Option 1: using devtools # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("ADC-405-S26/aRtpalette")  # Option 2: using pak # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("ADC-405-S26/aRtpalette")

title: Art-Inspired Color Palette Tools
text: package includes artwork_palettes, table 20 color palettes extracted iconic artworks across art history. row one painting, five hex color codes metadata including artist, artwork title, art movement.
code: library(aRtpalette) library(ggplot2) data(artwork_palettes) head(artwork_palettes[, c("palette_name", "artist", "style", "hex1", "hex2", "hex3")]) #>        palette_name   artist              style    hex1    hex2    hex3 #> 1      starry_night Van Gogh Post-Impressionism #1a3431 #2b41a7 #6283c8 #> 2     bedroom_arles Van Gogh Post-Impressionism #374D8D #93A0CB #82A866 #> 3 self_portrait_hat Van Gogh Post-Impressionism #FBDC30 #A7A651 #E0BA7A #> 4        great_wave  Hokusai            Ukiyo-e #1F284C #2D4472 #6E6352 #> 5      water_lilies    Monet      Impressionism #9F4640 #4885A4 #395A92 #> 6     woman_parasol    Monet      Impressionism #82A4BC #4C7899 #2F5136

what: palette_from_hex() — store colors as a validated object
title: Art-Inspired Color Palette Tools
text: function validates every hex code input returns clear error message malformed. problems surface immediately rather silently breaking chart later.
code: row <- artwork_palettes[artwork_palettes$palette_name == "starry_night", ]  starry_night <- palette_from_hex(   hex_codes = c(row$hex1, row$hex2, row$hex3, row$hex4, row$hex5),   name      = "starry_night" )  starry_night #> $name #> [1] "starry_night" #>  #> $colors #> [1] "#1a3431" "#2b41a7" "#6283c8" "#ccc776" "#c7ad24" #>  #> attr(,"class") #> [1] "artpalette"

title: Art-Inspired Color Palette Tools
text: Seeing palette swatches applying real data saves back--forth guessing whether colors work together.
code: plot_palette(starry_night)

what: apply_palette() — expand for any chart size
title: Art-Inspired Color Palette Tools
text: ask colors palette contains, function interpolates existing colors, allowing take original color expand creatively chart size.
code: # Exactly 3 colors for a 3-group chart apply_palette(starry_night, n = 3) #> [1] "#1A3431" "#6283C8" "#C7AD24" # Expand to 8 colors via interpolation apply_palette(starry_night, n = 8) #> [1] "#1A3431" "#233B74" "#324AAB" "#5270BE" "#8096B0" "#BCBD81" "#C9BB52" #> [8] "#C7AD24"

title: Art-Inspired Color Palette Tools
text: pull palette directly artwork_palettes, preview swatches, apply chart, showing three functions working together one clean pipeline.  swatch view lets us confirm colors look right committing chart. prevent wasting time going back forth later process.  palette came artwork_palettes, previewed plot_palette(), expanded fit number style groups apply_palette(), applied directly chart. hex codes typed hand.
code: # Step 1 — pull the Starry Night row from our dataset # and turn it into a validated palette object row <- artwork_palettes[artwork_palettes$palette_name == "starry_night", ]  pal <- palette_from_hex(   c(row$hex1, row$hex2, row$hex3, row$hex4, row$hex5),   name = "starry_night" )  # Step 2 — preview the palette before using it # so we know exactly what colors we are working with plot_palette(pal) # Step 3 — count artworks by art style from our own dataset style_counts <- as.data.frame(table(artwork_palettes$style)) colnames(style_counts) <- c("style", "count")  # Step 4 — expand the palette to exactly the number of  # style groups we need using apply_palette() chart_colors <- apply_palette(pal, n = nrow(style_counts))  # Step 5 — plug the colors directly into ggplot2 ggplot(style_counts, aes(x = reorder(style, count),                           y = count,                           fill = style)) +   geom_bar(stat = "identity", width = 0.7) +   scale_fill_manual(values = chart_colors) +   coord_flip() +   labs(     title    = "Number of Artworks by Art Style",     subtitle = "Colors from Van Gogh's The Starry Night",     x        = "Art Style",     y        = "Count"   ) +   theme_classic() +   theme(legend.position = "none")

title: Art-Inspired Color Palette Tools
text: aRtpalette provides three functions work together pipeline: 1.palette_from_hex() — store hex codes artwork validated, named palette object 2.plot_palette() — preview palette color swatches applying chart 3.apply_palette() — extract expand palette exactly number colors chart needs. Combined built-artwork_palettes dataset 20 palettes iconic artworks, package makes easy bring art history R without manual hex code lookup.

title: Art-Inspired Color Palette Tools
text: See full vignette detailed walkthrough including palette comparison across art movements, complete art analysis workflow, ggplot2 examples:
code: vignette("getting-started-with-aRtpalette")
